title: What is GEO? A Marketer's Guide to Generative Engine Optimisation
excerpt: Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, and Perplexity AI are changing how buyers discover brands. GEO is the new discipline that gets you cited — not just ranked.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Generative Engine Optimisation (GEO) is the practice of structuring your brand's content, authority signals, and technical data so that AI-powered search systems — including Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, Perplexity AI, and Microsoft Copilot — cite your brand in their generated responses. Unlike traditional SEO, GEO is not about ranking on a results page. It is about being selected as a trusted source by a language model writing answers on behalf of users.

Why GEO Matters for Brands in Southeast Asia in 2025

AI-generated search responses now appear on over 40% of Google searches in markets where they have rolled out. Perplexity AI handles more than 100 million queries per week. A 2024 Gartner study projects that traditional search engine volume will drop by 25% by 2026 as AI interfaces absorb early-stage research. For B2B brands across Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand, and South Korea, this means buyers are researching your category inside an AI interface before they ever visit your website. If your brand is not cited in those responses, you are invisible in the earliest stage of the buying journey.

How GEO Differs from Traditional SEO

SEO optimises for visibility on a ranked list. GEO optimises for inclusion in a generated answer. The underlying mechanics differ significantly. SEO relies on backlinks, keyword density, and page authority. GEO relies on entity clarity, content structure, and citation patterns that language models can extract and trust. A brand that ranks second in Google may still be cited first in a ChatGPT response if its content is more directly structured around answerable questions.

The Five Pillars of GEO Strategy

  • Entity Clarity — consistent brand identity across Wikipedia, Wikidata, Google Knowledge Graph, Crunchbase, and industry directories
  • Citation-Ready Content — short, declarative sentences; direct answers within the first 150 words; H2 and H3 headings framed as questions
  • Authority Signal Building — earned mentions in industry publications, conference proceedings, and partner press releases
  • Answer Engine Optimisation (AEO) — content structured around natural-language questions with 40–60 word answers beneath each heading
  • Technical Schema Markup — FAQPage, HowTo, Organization, and Article schemas that give AI systems machine-readable context

Measuring GEO Performance

Traditional SEO metrics only partially capture GEO impact. You also need to measure AI citation rate (how often your brand appears when relevant queries run across ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews), share of voice in AI-generated answers, and downstream branded search uplift. AKA Digital uses the Hashmeta.ai GEO platform to track brand visibility across AI search engines for clients across Southeast Asia.

Frequently Asked Questions About GEO

What is Generative Engine Optimisation (GEO)?

GEO is the practice of optimising brand content so that AI-powered search engines — such as Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, and Perplexity AI — cite your brand when generating answers to relevant queries. It combines SEO, AEO, and structured data best practices to improve AI citation frequency.

How is GEO different from SEO?

SEO targets ranked positions on a search results page. GEO targets inclusion in AI-generated answer text. GEO prioritises entity authority, structured content, and direct answers over keyword density and backlink volume.

How do I start with GEO for my business in Vietnam or Southeast Asia?

Start with a GEO audit: run 20–30 queries relevant to your category across ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews. Note which brands are cited. Then audit your own site against the five GEO pillars — entity clarity, content structure, authority signals, AEO formatting, and schema markup. title: CDP vs CRM: Which Does Your Brand Actually Need in 2025?
excerpt: Most brands have a CRM. Far fewer have a CDP — and many confuse the two. Here's the definitive guide to understanding the difference and deciding what your stack actually needs.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
A Customer Data Platform (CDP) and a Customer Relationship Management system (CRM) solve different problems. A CRM manages known customer and sales relationships. A CDP unifies behavioural data across all digital touchpoints to enable real-time personalisation. Most enterprise brands in Southeast Asia need both — but almost every team that has only one believes it is sufficient.

What is a CRM and What Does It Do?

A CRM platform — such as Salesforce, HubSpot, or Freshworks — is primarily a sales and service tool. It stores contact records, manages sales pipelines, tracks customer interactions, and supports customer service workflows. CRMs are designed around known, identified contacts. They are excellent at managing post-sale relationships and enabling sales teams to close deals, but they were not built to handle anonymous digital behaviour at scale.

What is a CDP and What Does It Do?

A Customer Data Platform — such as mParticle, Segment, or Mixpanel — ingests data from every digital touchpoint: websites, mobile apps, email platforms, ad networks, and offline events. It creates a unified customer profile that links anonymous visitors to known users across devices and sessions. CDPs process behavioural events in real time, enabling personalised experiences at scale. They are the data foundation that allows marketing teams to act on who someone is, not just who they said they were when they last filled in a form.

CDP vs CRM: Key Differences

DimensionCRMCDP
Primary userSales & ServiceMarketing & Data
Data typeContacts, deals, notesEvents, behaviours, identities
Real-time?RarelyNative
Anonymous profiles?NoYes
Activation channelsEmail, phone, CRM flowsAny digital channel

When Do You Need Both?

If you are an enterprise brand running omnichannel marketing across Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, or Thailand, you need both. Your CRM manages the sales relationship and post-sale service. Your CDP handles real-time personalisation, audience segmentation, and cross-channel marketing activation. AKA Digital implements both — including the data integration layer that keeps your CRM and CDP in sync.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the main difference between a CDP and a CRM?

A CRM manages known customer relationships for sales and service teams. A CDP unifies behavioural data from all digital touchpoints to create real-time customer profiles for marketing activation. CDPs handle anonymous users; CRMs do not.

Which should a Southeast Asia brand implement first — CRM or CDP?

Start with a CRM if you have an active sales team managing customer relationships. Add a CDP when you need to personalise marketing at scale across web, app, email, and paid channels — typically once you are generating significant digital traffic and need to unify your data.

title: First-Party Data Strategy: 5 Steps to Cookieless Marketing
excerpt: Third-party cookies are dead. Brands that own their own customer data will win the next decade. Here's the 5-step playbook AKA Digital uses with enterprise clients across Southeast Asia.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
First-party data strategy is the practice of collecting, unifying, and activating data that customers share directly with your brand — through website visits, app usage, email interactions, and purchase transactions. As third-party cookies phase out across browsers and regulators tighten data privacy rules across Southeast Asia, brands that build first-party data infrastructure now will have a structural competitive advantage by 2026.

Why First-Party Data is the Most Valuable Asset in Modern Marketing

First-party data is more accurate, more privacy-compliant, and more actionable than any third-party alternative. It reflects real customer behaviour with your brand — not inferred behaviour from other sites. Brands with strong first-party data assets see 2–4x better targeting performance in paid media campaigns than those relying on third-party segments. This gap widens as cookie-based targeting degrades further.

Step 1: Audit Your Current First-Party Data Assets

Most enterprise brands in Vietnam and Southeast Asia have more first-party data than they realise — transaction records from ERP systems, email subscriber lists, mobile app user databases, loyalty programme enrollments, and website behaviour logs. The challenge is not collection but unification. Map every data source and identify the customer identifier keys available in each system.

Step 2: Implement Unified Customer Identity

Choose a primary customer identifier — email is most common for B2C; company email and phone for B2B. Deploy a Customer Data Platform (CDP) that resolves identities across systems, linking an anonymous web visitor cookie ID to a known CRM contact the moment they sign in or make a purchase. This unified profile is the foundation for all personalisation.

Step 3: Build Zero-Party Data Collection

Zero-party data is information customers voluntarily share: preferences, communication opt-ins, and interest signals. Build structured collection touchpoints — preference centres, interactive content, loyalty enrolment forms, and post-purchase surveys. This data is the most valuable because it reflects expressed intent and is fully privacy-compliant under Vietnam's PDPD and Singapore's PDPA.

Step 4: Activate First-Party Audiences Across All Channels

Upload unified customer segments to paid media platforms as Customer Match lists (Google, Meta, LinkedIn, TikTok) and as seed audiences for lookalike modelling. First-party audiences consistently outperform third-party data segments by 2–4x in conversion rate — because they are people who already know and trust your brand.

Step 5: Measure, Attribute, and Iterate

Implement multi-touch attribution that captures the full customer journey — not just last-click conversion. Track customer lifetime value by acquisition source. The brands that invest in this data infrastructure in 2025 will compound an enormous advantage through 2027 and beyond.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is first-party data in digital marketing?

First-party data is information collected directly from customers through your own channels — your website, app, email list, CRM, and purchase history. It is the most accurate and privacy-compliant form of customer data available to marketers.

How does first-party data strategy help with cookieless marketing?

As third-party cookies phase out, first-party data becomes the primary basis for targeting, personalisation, and audience activation. Brands with unified first-party data assets can still deliver relevant experiences without relying on third-party cookie networks.

title: Programmatic DOOH in Southeast Asia: The Complete 2025 Guide
excerpt: Digital out-of-home advertising has gone programmatic — and Southeast Asia is one of the fastest-growing markets. Here's everything enterprise marketers need to know.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Programmatic Digital Out-of-Home (pDOOH) advertising is the real-time, data-driven buying and serving of ads on digital screens — from airport displays and mall media to street furniture and transit networks — using demand-side platforms (DSPs) and open programmatic exchanges. Southeast Asia is one of the world's fastest-growing pDOOH markets, with Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, and Thailand adding significant digital screen inventory every year.

What Makes Programmatic DOOH Different from Traditional OOH?

Traditional OOH is bought in fixed placements for fixed periods — a billboard for four weeks, a static price. Programmatic DOOH is purchased in real-time impressions through a DSP, with full control over when, where, and to whom your creative appears. You can buy by time of day, trigger creatives based on weather or sports results, pause and reallocate budget in real time, and retarget mobile users who passed your screen with follow-up digital ads.

The Southeast Asia pDOOH Landscape in 2025

Vietnam now has one of the fastest-growing digital screen inventories in the region, with premium placements covering malls, airports, street furniture, and transit in Ho Chi Minh City and Hanoi. Singapore commands the highest CPM rates but reaches the highest-value B2B audiences in the region. Indonesia's pDOOH growth is concentrated in Greater Jakarta. Thailand and Malaysia are expanding rapidly through mall network digitisation.

How to Run a Programmatic DOOH Campaign

  • Plan with audience data — use mobile location data to identify which screen locations index highest for your target customer profile
  • Buy via DSP — AKA Digital runs pDOOH through Google DV360 and specialist pDOOH DSPs with access to regional inventory
  • Trigger by context — weather, time of day, sports events, or live data feeds for dynamic creative
  • Measure with mobile attribution — use device ID matching to measure foot traffic uplift and mobile retargeting reach from DOOH exposure

Frequently Asked Questions

What is programmatic DOOH advertising?

Programmatic DOOH is the automated, data-driven buying of digital out-of-home advertising in real time through a DSP. It allows advertisers to target specific locations, times, and contexts — and to measure campaign performance with the precision of digital advertising.

Which DSPs support pDOOH in Southeast Asia?

Google DV360, The Trade Desk, and specialist pDOOH platforms such as Hivestack and Broadsign support programmatic digital out-of-home buying across Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand, and Malaysia. AKA Digital manages pDOOH campaigns across all these markets.

How much does a programmatic DOOH campaign cost in Vietnam?

pDOOH pricing in Vietnam varies by screen location, audience index, and time of day. Airport and premium mall inventory commands higher CPMs. Street-level networks offer broader reach at lower cost. Contact AKA Digital for a tailored media plan and cost estimate for your campaign objectives.

title: KOL Marketing in Vietnam: How to Measure ROI (Not Just Reach)
excerpt: Vietnam has one of Southeast Asia's most active KOL ecosystems — but most brands measure the wrong things. Here's how to run influencer campaigns that actually impact revenue.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
KOL (Key Opinion Leader) marketing in Vietnam delivers some of the highest consumer trust scores in Southeast Asia — particularly on TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram. Vietnamese consumers act on creator recommendations at unusually high rates. Yet most brands investing in KOL campaigns cannot answer one basic question: did it drive measurable business results? Here is the framework AKA Digital uses with clients to shift KOL measurement from vanity to value.

Why Most KOL Campaigns Are Measured Incorrectly

Most KOL campaign reports lead with impressions, reach, views, and engagement rate. These are brand awareness signals — useful for tracking, but not for revenue attribution. The problem is that brands treat them as performance metrics and make budget allocation decisions on incomplete data. A KOL with 2 million followers but 0.5% engagement rate may generate far less actual conversion than a micro-KOL with 50,000 highly engaged followers in your product category.

A Framework for KOL Revenue Attribution in Vietnam

  • Unique discount codes — every KOL gets a tracked code (e.g., CREATOR10) that captures exact conversion data per creator from use through to purchase
  • UTM-tagged links — every link in bio, story swipe-up, or video description uses unique UTM parameters that flow through to your analytics platform and CRM
  • Pixel retargeting — build custom audiences of people who visited from a specific KOL's content, retarget them separately, and measure conversion lift
  • Audience authentication — before spending any budget, verify KOL audience quality for fake followers, engagement pod activity, and demographic alignment with your target market

KOL Tier Strategy for Vietnamese Brands

Macro KOLs (1M+ followers) drive awareness and social proof. Micro KOLs (10K–100K) drive higher engagement and purchase intent within niche communities. Nano KOLs (under 10K) drive trust-based conversion at a fraction of macro rates. Best-performing campaigns in Vietnam combine all three tiers with budget weighted by your primary KPI — awareness, consideration, or conversion.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a KOL in Vietnamese marketing?

A KOL (Key Opinion Leader) in Vietnam is a content creator or public figure with a dedicated following who influences their audience's purchasing decisions. Vietnamese KOLs are active across TikTok, YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram across beauty, fashion, food, finance, and tech categories.

How do you measure KOL marketing ROI in Vietnam?

Measure KOL ROI using unique discount codes per creator, UTM-tracked links through to conversion, pixel-based retargeting audiences, and post-campaign branded search uplift. Combine these into a cost-per-acquisition (CPA) calculation per KOL to identify which creators drive actual business results.

What KOL marketing tools does AKA Digital use for Vietnamese clients?

AKA Digital uses audience analytics tools to verify KOL quality before engagement, UTM and pixel tracking through Mixpanel and CleverTap for conversion attribution, and campaign dashboards that report CPA, ROAS, and LTV contribution by creator. Talk to our social team about your KOL strategy.

title: The MarTech Stack for SEA Enterprise Brands in 2025
excerpt: With 10,000+ MarTech solutions globally, building the right stack for a Southeast Asian enterprise is harder than ever. Here's AKA Digital's framework for a stack that delivers.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
The right MarTech stack for a Southeast Asian enterprise brand is not a copy of what works in the United States or Europe. SEA markets are defined by mobile dominance (60–80% of digital engagement happens in-app), platform fragmentation across 12+ countries and 8+ languages, and consumer behaviours — including cash-on-delivery preferences in Vietnam and Indonesia — that break attribution models built for Western credit-card-first markets. Here is AKA Digital's recommended four-layer stack architecture for regional enterprise brands in 2025.

Why the SEA MarTech Market is Structurally Different

Three factors make Southeast Asia unique: fragmentation across markets with dramatically different consumer behaviour, mobile-first digital engagement that pushes the majority of interactions into apps rather than browsers, and payment method diversity that requires attribution approaches tailored to the region. A stack built on assumptions from North American or European deployments will underperform. Every platform selection and integration decision must be validated for SEA-specific data patterns.

The Four-Layer MarTech Stack Architecture

  • Layer 1 — Data Foundation: A CDP such as mParticle or Mixpanel that unifies customer data from all touchpoints and markets. Without this layer, everything else is built on disconnected data.
  • Layer 2 — Engagement & Automation: A cross-channel engagement platform — CleverTap, MoEngage, or Netcore Cloud — for push, email, SMS, in-app, and WhatsApp campaigns.
  • Layer 3 — Acquisition & Retargeting: Google DV360 for programmatic plus Meta, TikTok, and LinkedIn for social — all activated with first-party audience data.
  • Layer 4 — Optimisation & Intelligence: A/B testing (AB Tasty), mobile attribution (Adjust), and business intelligence (Looker Studio or Mixpanel dashboards).

Three Mistakes Enterprise Brands Make with MarTech in Southeast Asia

The first is over-buying point solutions. The average enterprise team uses 14–18 MarTech tools and underutilises most of them. Consolidate around a unified CDP before adding more disconnected tools. The second is underinvesting in implementation quality — 80% of MarTech failures are implementation failures, not platform failures. The third is measuring platform adoption instead of business outcomes. The right question is not are our teams using this tool — it is has CPA decreased, has LTV increased, is churn lower.

Frequently Asked Questions

What MarTech stack does AKA Digital recommend for Vietnam?

For Vietnam enterprise brands, AKA Digital recommends Mixpanel or mParticle as the CDP foundation, CleverTap or MoEngage for engagement automation, Google DV360 for programmatic, and Adjust for mobile attribution. The exact stack depends on your business model, team size, and existing integrations.

How much does a MarTech stack cost for a Southeast Asian enterprise?

Enterprise MarTech investment varies significantly by scale. A mid-market stack (CDP + engagement + attribution) starts from USD 30,000–80,000 annually in platform licences, plus implementation costs. Mixpanel's event-based data model is built for mobile app behaviour — tracking user actions rather than page views, making it ideal for markets where most digital engagement happens inside apps. AKA Digital is Vietnam's exclusive Mixpanel representative. Book a free Mixpanel demo.

title: Key Tools for Product Analytics Success
excerpt: Product analytics provides the insights needed to make data-driven decisions that enhance user experience and increase retention. Choosing the right tools — and using them correctly — makes all the difference.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Product analytics tools enable organisations to track user behaviour in real time, segment users based on actions and demographics, and identify exactly where products succeed or fail in the user journey. The right product analytics platform transforms raw behavioural data into decisions that improve retention, reduce churn, and increase feature adoption. For product teams in Southeast Asia, where mobile-first usage dominates and user expectations evolve rapidly, analytics is not optional — it is the operating system of product improvement.

Why You Need the Right Tools for Product Analytics

Without proper analytics implementation, data collection becomes incomplete or inaccurate, leading to decisions based on assumption rather than evidence. A SaaS company without product analytics cannot see which features drive engagement, where users abandon onboarding, or which user segments are most likely to convert to paid plans. In competitive markets across Vietnam, Singapore, and Indonesia, product teams that act on real behavioural data ship better products faster than those that rely on intuition.

Seven Essential Features to Look for in Product Analytics Tools

  • Event Tracking — monitoring specific user actions: clicks, form submissions, feature interactions, and video plays
  • User Segmentation — grouping users by behaviour, demographics, device, or geography for targeted analysis
  • Funnel Analysis — visualising conversion steps and identifying where users drop off before completing goals
  • Retention Analysis — measuring how frequently users return over time, identifying churn patterns and loyalty drivers
  • Integration Capabilities — connecting with CRM, marketing automation, and customer support tools for full journey visibility
  • Customisable Dashboards — reporting tailored to each team's priorities, from product managers to C-suite
  • Data Privacy Compliance — adherence to GDPR, Vietnam's PDPD, Singapore's PDPA, and other regional frameworks

Leading Product Analytics Tools in 2025

Mixpanel is renowned for event tracking and funnel analysis, making it the recommended tool for mobile-first markets across Southeast Asia. It excels at real-time user segmentation and retention cohort analysis. AKA Digital is Vietnam's exclusive Mixpanel representative. Amplitude offers strong behavioural cohort analysis and predictive capabilities. Google Analytics 4 provides unified web and app measurement with event-based tracking and integrates with Google's advertising ecosystem. Heap captures all user interactions automatically without manual tagging, enabling retroactive analysis.

How to Maximise the Value of Your Analytics Investment

Define clear objectives before implementation — what business questions do you need to answer? Establish consistent event naming conventions across your engineering and product teams. Schedule regular data review cadences: weekly for product decisions, monthly for strategic planning. Enable cross-team access so marketing, sales, and customer success can all act on insights. Invest in team training — analytics tools only generate value when teams know how to use them and act on what they find.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is product analytics?

Product analytics is the practice of collecting, measuring, and analysing data about how users interact with a digital product — website, mobile app, or software platform. It provides the insights that guide product development decisions: which features to build, fix, or remove; how to improve onboarding; and how to increase user retention.

AKA Digital is Vietnam's exclusive representative and provides local implementation and support.

title: Drive Innovation with Advanced MarTech Solutions
excerpt: Advanced marketing technology tools help companies streamline marketing, gain customer insights, and deliver personalised experiences at scale. Here's how to choose and implement them effectively.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Advanced marketing technology platforms deliver measurable competitive advantages for brands that implement and operate them correctly. The core benefits are improved efficiency through task automation, enhanced customer understanding via unified data collection, the ability to deliver personalised content at scale based on real behavioural segments, and superior ROI measurement that makes every marketing investment accountable. For enterprise brands in Southeast Asia, advanced MarTech is no longer a competitive edge — it is the operating baseline.

Understanding the Impact of Marketing Technology

Marketing technology encompasses the software platforms that power every stage of the customer journey — from CRM systems that manage relationships to CDP platforms that unify behavioural data, to engagement tools that automate personalised messaging across push, email, SMS, and in-app channels. When properly integrated, these tools enable a single team to manage personalised communication at a scale that would require hundreds of people without technology. A company using integrated CRM and marketing automation can automatically send personalised follow-ups based on specific customer behaviours — without manual intervention.

How to Choose the Right MarTech Tools for Your Business

Begin with business objectives, not platform features. The most common mistake is evaluating tools based on demo experience rather than fit with your data architecture, team capabilities, and market context. A platform that works brilliantly for a US SaaS company may be entirely wrong for a Vietnamese financial services brand. Key evaluation criteria: compatibility with your existing technology infrastructure; real scalability validated by comparable deployments; total cost of ownership including implementation and ongoing management; and the quality of local or regional support.

Leveraging Data Analytics for Smarter Marketing Decisions

Modern MarTech platforms enable capabilities that were impossible even five years ago: customer segmentation down to the individual level; predictive modelling of churn probability and lifetime value; real-time KPI monitoring across the full marketing funnel; and A/B testing at scale across product experiences and marketing messages. These capabilities compound over time — brands with three years of clean first-party data have a structural advantage over those starting today.

Integration: The Make-or-Break Factor

Connected systems eliminate data fragmentation and enable automated workflows across CRM, email, social, and analytics. The most common failure pattern in MarTech deployments: organisations invest in excellent individual tools but never achieve the integration that makes them collectively valuable. Unified data architecture is not a nice-to-have — it is the entire point of a modern marketing technology stack.

title: From Push Ads to Personalised Engagement: The Evolution of Mobile Marketing
excerpt: The era of blanket push notifications is over. Here's how enterprise mobile marketing has evolved from intrusive broadcast to intelligent, personalised engagement — and what that means for your brand.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
The first wave of mobile marketing was defined by volume: blast as many push notifications as possible, hope that some convert. The result was predictable — notification fatigue, app uninstalls, and regulatory backlash. The second wave — now underway across Southeast Asia — is defined by intelligence: contextual, personalised engagement that respects user preferences and delivers value at every touchpoint. Brands making this transition are seeing dramatically higher retention and lifetime value from their mobile audiences.

Why Broadcast Push Notifications Fail in 2025

Users in Vietnam, Indonesia, and Thailand receive an average of 46 app notifications per day across all their installed apps. Research consistently shows that non-personalised push notifications have opt-out rates exceeding 60% within the first month. Every irrelevant notification erodes trust. Every opt-out reduces future reach. The economics of broadcast push marketing are fundamentally broken in markets where users have more choices and less patience than ever before.

The Shift to Intelligent, Personalised Engagement

Personalised mobile engagement is built on four capabilities: behavioural data that reflects what individual users actually do in your app; segmentation that groups users by behaviour, lifecycle stage, and preference; contextual triggers that send messages based on specific actions or inactions; and intelligent timing that delivers messages when individual users are most likely to engage. Platforms like CleverTap and MoEngage provide all four — enabling brands to replace broadcast with dialogue.

Key Engagement Channels Beyond Push in 2025

  • In-App Messages — contextual messages shown while the user is active in the app, triggered by specific actions
  • WhatsApp Business API — increasingly important in Vietnam, Indonesia, and Malaysia for transactional and promotional messages
  • Email Automation — behaviour-triggered sequences that nurture users through key lifecycle stages
  • SMS — high open rates in markets with strong feature phone penetration, including parts of Vietnam and Indonesia
  • Web Push — extending personalised engagement beyond the app to mobile browsers

How to Build a Personalised Mobile Engagement Strategy

Start by defining your user lifecycle stages — from install through first transaction, through repeat purchase, through loyalty. Map the key behavioural signals at each stage (first app open, first purchase, 7-day inactivity). Design engagement flows for each transition. Implement A/B testing on message content, timing, and frequency. Measure engagement lift, conversion rate, and retention impact — not just open rate. Continuously refine based on what the data shows.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between push ads and personalised mobile engagement?

Push ads are broadcast notifications sent to all users or broad segments regardless of individual behaviour or preference. Personalised mobile engagement uses behavioural data and intelligent automation to send contextually relevant messages to specific users at moments when those messages are most likely to be valuable and welcome.

title: 7 Strategies to Incentivise Customer Loyalty with Data-Driven Marketing
excerpt: Customer acquisition costs 5–7x more than retention. Here are seven proven strategies for building loyalty programmes powered by data — deployed by leading brands across Southeast Asia.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Customer loyalty programmes powered by data outperform traditional points-based systems by an order of magnitude. When loyalty mechanics are connected to real behavioural data — purchase history, app engagement, category preferences, lifecycle stage — brands can deliver incentives that feel genuinely relevant rather than generic. Across Vietnam, Singapore, and Indonesia, enterprise brands using data-driven loyalty strategies report 25–40% improvements in repeat purchase rate and 20–35% increases in customer lifetime value over 12 months.

Strategy 1: Segment Loyalty Benefits by Behaviour, Not Spend

Traditional loyalty tiers reward total spend. Data-driven loyalty rewards specific behaviours — frequency, category diversity, referral activity, content engagement. A customer who buys the same product monthly is less valuable than a customer who tries new categories and refers friends. Design tier benefits that incentivise the behaviours most correlated with lifetime value in your specific business.

Strategy 2: Personalise Rewards to Individual Preferences

Use purchase history and browsing data to offer rewards that align with each customer's demonstrated preferences. A customer who buys sportswear should receive loyalty perks in sportswear, not kitchen appliances. Mixpanel and CleverTap enable dynamic personalisation of loyalty offers based on real behavioural segments.

Strategy 3: Use Surprise-and-Delight at Predictive Churn Points

Machine learning models can predict churn risk based on declining engagement patterns. A customer who has not purchased in 45 days and whose session frequency has dropped is exhibiting churn signals. Trigger a personalised surprise reward — an exclusive offer, early access, or bonus points — before they lapse, not after. Proactive retention is always less expensive than win-back.

Strategy 4: Build Community Around Your Loyalty Programme

Loyalty programmes that include social and community elements see significantly higher engagement than purely transactional systems. Create exclusive community spaces (loyalty member groups on Zalo or Facebook in Vietnam; LINE communities in Thailand) where top-tier members share experiences, get early access to products, and connect with the brand on a human level.

Strategy 5: Gamify the Loyalty Journey

Progress mechanics — streaks, challenges, badges, milestone rewards — dramatically increase engagement with loyalty programmes. A user who has completed 8 of 10 challenges to reach Gold tier is highly motivated to complete the remaining two. Design your loyalty programme with visible progress indicators and time-limited challenges to sustain engagement.

Strategy 6: Integrate Loyalty Data with Your CRM and CDP

Loyalty data is only valuable when it flows into your full marketing stack. Integrate your loyalty platform with your CRM (Salesforce, Freshworks) and CDP (mParticle, Mixpanel) so that loyalty status, tier, and behaviour data enriches every customer interaction across all channels — from personalised push notifications to sales team conversations.

Strategy 7: Measure Loyalty Programme ROI with LTV Attribution

Measure loyalty programme success by customer lifetime value contribution — not just points redeemed or members enrolled. Track LTV by loyalty tier, identify the tier transitions that drive the most value, and calculate the cost of each loyalty benefit against the incremental revenue generated. Data-driven loyalty optimisation is an ongoing discipline, not a one-time design exercise.

Frequently Asked Questions

What makes a loyalty programme data-driven?

A data-driven loyalty programme uses customer behavioural data — purchase history, app engagement, product preferences, lifecycle stage — to personalise rewards, trigger incentives at optimal moments, predict churn risk, and continuously optimise programme mechanics based on measured outcomes rather than assumptions.

title: 7 Email Marketing Campaigns Every App Should Run Across the User Lifecycle
excerpt: Email remains the highest-ROI channel for mobile app retention when executed with behavioural triggers. Here are the 7 lifecycle campaigns every app team should have running.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Email marketing delivers the highest ROI of any digital channel — averaging USD 36 for every USD 1 spent — when campaigns are triggered by real user behaviour rather than scheduled broadcast. For mobile apps across Southeast Asia, a properly implemented lifecycle email programme reduces churn, drives feature adoption, and recovers lapsed users at a fraction of the cost of paid re-acquisition. Here are the seven campaigns every app should have running.

Campaign 1: Welcome Series (Day 0–3)

The first 72 hours after install determine whether a user becomes active or dormant. A three-part welcome series — triggered immediately on registration — should introduce your app's core value proposition, highlight the single most important action the user should take first, and provide social proof that builds confidence. Keep each email focused on one action. Users who complete the key onboarding action in the first 3 days have 2–3x the 30-day retention rate of those who do not.

Campaign 2: Onboarding Activation (Day 3–14)

Onboarding email campaigns guide users to their first meaningful outcome — the moment when they experience the core value of your product. Trigger these based on what users have and have not done: a user who has completed profile setup but not their first transaction needs a different email than a user who has made one purchase but not set up notifications.

Campaign 3: Feature Adoption

Most app users discover and use only a fraction of available features. Feature adoption emails — triggered when users have not engaged with a high-value feature within a set window — drive discovery without requiring in-app real estate. Keep these educational, benefit-focused, and tied to what you know about the user's behaviour.

Campaign 4: Milestone and Achievement Celebration

Emails celebrating user milestones — first purchase, 10th session, 30-day anniversary, loyalty tier upgrade — drive emotional connection and reinforce positive behaviour loops. These emails have open rates 2–4x higher than standard promotional emails because they are personally relevant and positive.

Campaign 5: Re-engagement (Lapse Prevention)

Trigger re-engagement emails when users show early lapse signals — typically 7–14 days of inactivity depending on your app's expected usage frequency. Include a clear reason to return: a new feature, a personalised offer, or a reminder of value they are missing. A/B test subject lines aggressively — subject line is the primary determinant of whether a lapsed user opens at all.

Campaign 6: Win-Back (Post-Lapse)

Win-back campaigns target users who have been inactive for 30–90 days. These require a stronger incentive than re-engagement campaigns — a meaningful offer, exclusive content, or a significant product update. Accept that win-back conversion rates are lower than retention rates; the goal is to recover the subset of lapsed users who still have intent to return.

Campaign 7: Loyalty and Advocacy

Your most engaged users are your best acquisition channel. Automated emails to high-frequency, high-value users requesting reviews, referrals, and user-generated content convert at rates far higher than cold acquisition. Identify your top decile of users by engagement score and invest in nurturing them as advocates.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is app lifecycle email marketing?

App lifecycle email marketing is the practice of sending behaviour-triggered emails at key stages of the user journey — from welcome through onboarding, activation, retention, re-engagement, and advocacy. Lifecycle emails are more effective than broadcast campaigns because they are contextually relevant to where each user is in their relationship with your product.

title: Understanding Sender Reputation: The Key to Email Deliverability
excerpt: Your email content is only as valuable as your ability to reach the inbox. Here's what sender reputation is, why it matters, and how to protect it — particularly in Southeast Asian markets.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Email sender reputation is a score assigned by Internet Service Providers (ISPs) and mailbox providers — Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook — to your sending domain and IP address. This score determines whether your emails reach the inbox, land in spam, or are blocked entirely. No amount of creative excellence or personalisation matters if your emails do not reach their destination. For enterprise brands sending marketing and transactional emails across Vietnam, Singapore, and Indonesia, protecting sender reputation is a critical operational discipline.

What Factors Determine Sender Reputation?

Sender reputation is calculated from multiple signals. Spam complaint rate — the percentage of recipients who mark your email as spam — is the single most damaging factor. Google recommends keeping complaint rates below 0.10%; rates above 0.30% trigger deliverability problems. Other key factors include bounce rate (hard bounces from invalid addresses damage reputation significantly), engagement rate (opens and clicks signal to ISPs that recipients want your email), unsubscribe rate, and sending volume consistency. Sudden spikes in sending volume trigger spam filters even for senders with good reputations.

How to Build and Protect Sender Reputation

  • Use double opt-in — confirms subscriber intent and dramatically reduces complaint rates
  • Maintain list hygiene — remove hard bounces immediately and suppress inactive subscribers after 6–12 months of non-engagement
  • Authenticate your domain — implement SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records for all sending domains
  • Warm up new IPs — gradually increase sending volume on new IP addresses over 4–8 weeks
  • Monitor spam traps — old email addresses repurposed as spam traps will damage reputation if they remain on your list
  • Segment engagement levels — send to your most engaged subscribers first; suppress low-engagement contacts from broad campaigns

Monitoring Your Sender Reputation

Use Google Postmaster Tools (for Gmail deliverability), Microsoft SNDS (for Outlook), and MxToolbox to monitor your domain and IP reputation in real time. These tools show spam complaint rates, IP reputation scores, and authentication status. Set up alerts for any reputation degradation so you can respond before significant deliverability impact occurs.

Recovering from Deliverability Problems

If your emails are landing in spam, begin with a thorough list audit: remove all addresses with recent hard bounces, suppress contacts who have not opened in 12+ months, and verify that authentication (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) is correctly configured. Then re-engage only your highest-engagement segment and gradually rebuild volume. Recovery typically takes 4–8 weeks of disciplined sending to non-spammers.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is email sender reputation?

Email sender reputation is a trust score assigned by mailbox providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook) to your sending domain and IP address. It determines whether your emails reach the inbox or are filtered to spam. It is based on complaint rates, bounce rates, engagement signals, and sending consistency.

How do I check my email sender reputation?

Check sender reputation using Google Postmaster Tools for Gmail deliverability data, Microsoft SNDS for Outlook/Hotmail insights, and MxToolbox for domain and IP reputation checks. Most enterprise email platforms (CleverTap, Netcore, SendGrid) also provide built-in deliverability monitoring dashboards.

What spam complaint rate is acceptable for email marketing?

Google recommends keeping spam complaint rates below 0.10%. Rates above 0.30% cause Gmail to throttle or block your emails. Most enterprise senders should target complaint rates below 0.05% through double opt-in, preference management, and regular list hygiene. Talk to AKA Digital about email deliverability strategy.

The Analytics Challenge for a Fashion Platform at Scale

Fashion eCommerce presents unique analytics challenges. Purchase intent is highly visual and emotional. Discovery pathways are non-linear — a shopper might find a product through search, browse similar items, add to wishlist, leave, return via a push notification, and finally purchase through a different device. Connecting these touchpoints into a coherent view of the customer journey requires event-based analytics that can track behaviour across sessions, devices, and time — capabilities where Mixpanel excels.

How Zalora Uses Mixpanel for Fashion Discovery Optimisation

Product discovery is the most critical metric in fashion eCommerce — a shopper who cannot find what they want in the first few minutes will leave. Zalora uses Mixpanel funnel analysis to identify exactly where the discovery experience breaks down: which search queries return unsatisfying results, which filter combinations lead to empty states, and which product categories have high browse-to-wishlist rates but low purchase conversion. Conversion Rate Optimisation with Behavioural Data

Zalora's product teams use Mixpanel to run structured A/B tests on every significant experience change — new product page layouts, updated checkout flows, revised size guide formats. By measuring conversion impact at the individual event level (product view → add to cart → checkout initiation → purchase completion), teams can identify exactly which design elements move the needle on revenue rather than relying on aggregate session metrics that obscure causal relationships.

Retention Analytics for Fashion Loyalty

Fashion shoppers are notoriously hard to retain — the next sale at a competitor is always one notification away. Zalora uses Mixpanel cohort analysis to understand the purchasing patterns of loyal shoppers: how many purchases within the first 90 days predict long-term retention, which product categories drive the highest repeat purchase rates, and which user segments are most at risk of churning to competitors. These retention insights inform both marketing automation and loyalty programme design.

Key Takeaways for Southeast Asian eCommerce Brands

Zalora's use of Mixpanel demonstrates three principles applicable to any eCommerce business in the region: measure every step of the purchase funnel, not just final conversion; use cohort analysis to understand what drives long-term customer loyalty; and run continuous A/B tests to validate that product improvements actually improve business outcomes before full rollout.

title: The Product Manager's Guide to Analytics: Making Better Decisions with Data
excerpt: Great product managers make decisions with data, not instinct alone. Here's the complete framework for using product analytics to build better products, improve retention, and drive growth.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Product analytics is the discipline that separates product managers who build features based on assumptions from those who build features based on evidence. By tracking how users actually interact with your product — what they do, where they stop, which features they adopt, and when they leave — product analytics replaces guesswork with data and transforms product roadmap decisions from internal debates into evidence-based choices. For PMs building digital products across Southeast Asia, analytics fluency is now as essential as user research skills.

Why Product Managers Need Analytics in 2025

The volume and velocity of product decisions in modern software development makes intuition insufficient. A PM managing a roadmap of 20+ features across a product used by hundreds of thousands of users in multiple markets cannot rely on qualitative research alone to prioritise. Analytics answers the questions that matter most: which features drive retention, which drive churn, which are used by your highest-value user segments, and which are invisible to most of your users despite significant engineering investment.

The Core Analytics Framework for Product Managers

Every product analytics practice should be built around four measurement pillars. Acquisition: where are users coming from and which sources generate the highest-quality users (measured by retention and conversion, not just volume)? Activation: what is the completion rate of your onboarding flow and what is the correlation between specific onboarding actions and 30-day retention? Retention: what percentage of users who installed your product 30, 60, and 90 days ago are still active today, and which cohorts retain best? Revenue: which user behaviours are most predictive of conversion to paid, upsell, and expansion revenue?

Key Mixpanel Features Every Product Manager Should Master

  • Funnel Analysis — visualise conversion rates between every step of a user journey and identify where users drop off
  • Retention Reports — measure how many users who completed a specific action return to the product over time
  • Cohort Analysis — group users by acquisition date, first action, or any property to compare retention across segments
  • User Flows — visualise the paths users take through your product to discover unexpected navigation patterns
  • A/B Testing — measure the impact of product changes on conversion and retention with statistical confidence

Building a Data-Driven Product Culture

The most analytically capable product teams share several practices: they define success metrics before building features (not after), they establish dashboards that give everyone — engineering, design, marketing — visibility into the same product metrics, they run retrospectives that compare predicted versus actual metric impact of shipped features, and they use data to kill features that are not working as ruthlessly as they use it to prioritise new ones.

Mixpanel is particularly popular in Southeast Asian mobile-first product teams for its event tracking and retention analysis capabilities.

Funnel Analysis for Purchase Flow Optimisation

For a platform at Ticketmaster's scale, even a 1% improvement in checkout conversion generates significant incremental revenue.

Mobile App Optimisation for Fan Engagement

The Ticketmaster mobile app is the primary channel for millions of fans who discover events, purchase tickets, and access their tickets at the venue. title: How Blue Wizard Digital Uses Mixpanel to Level Up Mobile Gaming Analytics
excerpt: Mobile gaming success is determined by player retention and monetisation — metrics that require precise behavioural analytics. Here's how Blue Wizard Digital uses Mixpanel to optimise both.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Blue Wizard Digital is a mobile games studio best known for Stupid Zombies, a casual mobile game with tens of millions of downloads worldwide. Mobile gaming is one of the most analytically intensive industries in the world — player acquisition costs are high, player attention is scarce, and the difference between a game that monetises successfully and one that fails often comes down to small optimisations in the core gameplay loop, onboarding, and in-app purchase presentation. Mixpanel provides Blue Wizard with the event-based analytics to make these optimisations with precision.

The Analytics Challenges of Mobile Gaming

Mobile game analytics must answer three core questions simultaneously: Are new players completing the onboarding experience? Are engaged players returning day after day? Are players who reach the monetisation points in the game actually converting to payers? Each of these questions requires different analytical approaches. Onboarding analysis uses funnel reports. Engagement analysis uses retention cohorts. Monetisation analysis uses event segmentation around in-app purchase events. Mixpanel handles all three natively.

Day-1, Day-7, and Day-30 Retention Analysis

Mobile gaming KPIs are structured around retention benchmarks: D1 (Day-1 retention — what percentage of players return the day after install), D7, and D30. Industry benchmarks vary by genre but casual games typically target D1 above 35%, D7 above 15%, and D30 above 5%. Blue Wizard uses Mixpanel's retention reporting to track these metrics in real time, segment by acquisition source, device type, and player geography to identify which cohorts retain best — and reverse-engineer what drove that retention.

In-App Purchase Funnel Optimisation

Mixpanel funnel analysis enables Blue Wizard to see exactly how players move from engaging with gameplay to viewing an in-app purchase offer to completing a transaction. Identifying where players abandon the purchase funnel — whether at the offer view, the product selection, or the payment confirmation step — enables precise UI optimisation that meaningfully improves conversion rates without changing the underlying game economics.

Implications for the Southeast Asia Gaming Market

Southeast Asia is one of the world's fastest-growing mobile gaming markets. Vietnam, Indonesia, Thailand, and the Philippines are home to hundreds of millions of mobile gamers. The analytics practices Blue Wizard applies to optimise player retention and monetisation are equally applicable to gaming companies building for Southeast Asian audiences — particularly given the region's diversity in device capability, connectivity, and spending behaviour.

Frequently Asked Questions

What analytics tools do mobile game studios use?

Mobile game studios use Mixpanel, Amplitude, and GameAnalytics for behavioural player analytics; AppsFlyer and Adjust for mobile attribution; and Firebase for basic app analytics. Mixpanel is particularly valued for its retention cohort analysis and funnel reporting, which directly map to the core KPIs in mobile gaming.

What is a good Day-1 retention rate for a mobile game?

Day-1 retention benchmarks vary by genre. Casual games target 35–40% D1 retention; mid-core games 30–35%; hardcore games 25–30%. Any game below 20% D1 retention needs significant onboarding improvement before investing in user acquisition at scale.

title: How Check City Uses Mixpanel to Improve Financial Service Conversions

author: AKA Digital Team

The Analytics Challenge in Consumer Financial Services

Financial services application funnels are uniquely complex. Aggregate analytics tools that measure only page-level conversion obscure the specific interaction patterns that cause applicants to leave. Funnel Analysis for Loan Application Optimisation

Each identified friction point becomes a targeted optimisation opportunity.

Behavioural Segmentation for Financial Product Matching

Not all applicants have the same financial profile or product fit. Mixpanel's segmentation capabilities enable Check City to understand how different user segments — defined by demographic data, application behaviour, and financial product interest — navigate the digital experience differently. These insights inform personalisation strategies: presenting the right product to each applicant based on their stated needs and browsing behaviour, which improves both conversion rate and customer satisfaction.

Lessons for Financial Services in Southeast Asia

Southeast Asia's financial services sector is undergoing rapid digital transformation. Digital lending, BNPL, insurance, and wealth management platforms across Vietnam, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand are competing aggressively for customer acquisition. title: How Uber Uses Mixpanel to Drive Product Intelligence at Scale
excerpt: Uber operates in 70+ countries across billions of trips. Here's how product intelligence and behavioural analytics power decision-making at one of the world's most data-driven technology companies.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Uber is one of the world's most data-intensive technology businesses. Operating across 70+ countries, supporting millions of trips daily, and serving both riders and drivers through a dual-sided marketplace, Uber's ability to make product decisions with precision at scale is a core competitive capability. Mixpanel has been part of the analytics infrastructure that Uber uses to understand user behaviour, optimise the rider and driver experience, and rapidly test product improvements across markets. The lessons from Uber's analytics practice are applicable to any digital business seeking to build product intelligence capabilities.

Analytics at the Scale of Uber

At Uber's scale, even a 0.1% improvement in rider conversion rate or driver utilisation represents enormous value. This creates extraordinary incentive to invest in precise, event-level product analytics — and to build the internal capabilities to act on those insights at the speed of software development cycles. Uber's product teams use analytics not just to measure what happened, but to understand why it happened and what should be changed as a result.

Rider Experience Optimisation

The rider experience spans multiple critical moments: app open, trip request, driver matching, ride tracking, payment, and rating. Each step is a potential source of friction — a slow match, an unclear map, a confusing payment screen — that affects both conversion in the moment and long-term rider retention. Mixpanel's event tracking enables product teams to identify and prioritise friction points based on their impact on the metrics that matter most: trip completion rate, rider retention, and Net Promoter Score.

Driver Experience and Supply-Side Analytics

Driver supply is the fundamental constraint in ride-sharing — without enough drivers in the right places at the right times, the rider experience degrades. Uber uses analytics to understand how drivers engage with the driver app, which features improve driver productivity and earnings predictability, and what onboarding experiences generate drivers who remain active for the longest time. These insights are as important to Uber's business as rider-side analytics.

Market Expansion Analytics

Uber has entered dozens of new markets over its history, and analytics plays a critical role in market entry strategy. Understanding rider and driver behaviour in new markets — how usage patterns differ from established markets, which product features require localisation, and what incentive levels are needed to achieve market liquidity — requires robust behavioural analytics from the earliest stages of market operation. Mixpanel's multi-market analytics capabilities support this global product intelligence function.

What Scale-Stage Companies Can Learn from Uber

While most businesses do not operate at Uber's scale, the analytics principles Uber applies are directly relevant. Define success metrics before building features. Instrument every significant user action. Use funnel analysis to identify conversion drop-offs. Use retention cohorts to understand what drives long-term engagement. Run A/B tests before full rollout. Build dashboards that make analytics accessible to every team — not just data scientists.

Frequently Asked Questions

What analytics tools does Uber use?

Uber uses a combination of internal data infrastructure and third-party analytics tools including Mixpanel for product behaviour analytics, along with custom-built data pipelines and business intelligence platforms. Product teams across Uber's global operations use Mixpanel for funnel analysis, retention reporting, and experiment measurement.

How does a ride-sharing platform measure product success?

Ride-sharing platforms measure success through trip completion rate, rider retention (trips per rider per month), driver utilisation rate, and market liquidity (time between trip request and driver match). Product improvements are evaluated against their impact on these metrics through A/B testing before global rollout.
